heroOverview: Pemphigus is a group of rare autoimmune blistering disorders in which the body produces IgG antibodies against desmosomal proteins that hold skin cells together, causing the outer layers of the skin and mucous membranes to separate and form fragile, painful blisters. The global incidence is roughly 1-5 cases per million people per year, with higher rates in certain ethnic groups including Ashkenazi Jews and people of Middle Eastern and Indian origin. The blisters rupture easily, leaving raw erosions that are prone to secondary infection and significant fluid loss. Before immunosuppressive therapy became available, pemphigus vulgaris carried a mortality of up to 75%; modern treatment with corticosteroids and rituximab has reduced mortality to approximately 5-10%, though the disease remains serious and requires long-term specialist management. Early recognition and prompt initiation of treatment are essential for controlling the disease before extensive erosions develop.

keyStats.prevalence: Incidence 1-5 per million per year globally; higher in Ashkenazi Jewish, Indian, and Middle Eastern populations where rates reach 16-32 per million per year
keyStats.demographics: Most commonly diagnosed in adults aged 40-60; pemphigus vulgaris accounts for approximately 70-80% of all pemphigus cases worldwide
keyStats.avgAge: Mean age of onset for pemphigus vulgaris is approximately 50-60 years; pemphigus foliaceous can occur at any age including childhood in endemic fogo selvagem regions
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 40,000-50,000 prevalent cases estimated worldwide; exact global burden is uncertain due to underdiagnosis in low-income settings

definition: Pemphigus (ICD-10: L10.9, unspecified) is a chronic autoimmune blistering skin disease characterised by intraepidermal acantholysis — separation of keratinocytes within the epidermis caused by antibodies against desmoglein 1 (Dsg1) and desmoglein 3 (Dsg3), which are transmembrane glycoproteins of the desmosome adhesion complex. In pemphigus vulgaris, the most prevalent form, antibodies predominantly target Dsg3, leading to suprabasal cleavage that produces flaccid blisters predominantly on mucous membranes and skin. In pemphigus foliaceous, antibodies target Dsg1 alone, producing superficial subcorneal blisters confined to the skin without mucosal involvement. IgG autoantibodies are detected in serum and in skin biopsy by direct and indirect immunofluorescence, showing the characteristic intercellular IgG deposition pattern described as a chicken-wire or fishnet appearance. The pathological hallmark on histology is an intraepidermal blister with acantholytic cells — Tzanck cells — at the base. Pemphigus falls under dermatology, though mucosal involvement may involve oral medicine, ophthalmology, and otolaryngology. It is not infectious or contagious and does not arise from an external allergen.

typesClassification.description: The most common form; anti-Dsg3 (and often anti-Dsg1) IgG antibodies cause suprabasal acantholysis, producing painful flaccid blisters and erosions on mucous membranes (oral, laryngeal, oesophageal, genital) and skin.
Anti-Dsg1 antibodies only; produces superficial, easily ruptured blisters in a seborrhoeic distribution on the face, scalp, and trunk with no mucosal involvement; generally less severe than PV.
An endemic form of PF concentrated in rural Brazil, triggered by environmental factors possibly including insect bites in genetically susceptible individuals; can present as childhood onset disease.
Caused by drugs including penicillamine, captopril, and other thiol or phenol compounds that modify desmoglein structure or trigger an autoimmune response; usually resolves after drug withdrawal.

The most severe and life-threatening variant; associated with underlying malignancy (most often lymphoma); antibodies target multiple plakin and desmoglein proteins causing severe mucocutaneous blistering and obliterative bronchiolitis.
An overlap between pemphigus foliaceous and lupus erythematosus; anti-Dsg1 antibodies plus antinuclear antibodies; confined to the face and upper trunk in a malar distribution.

primarySymptoms: Painful oral erosions, often the first manifestation of pemphigus vulgaris — burning ulcers on the gingiva, palate, buccal mucosa, or tongue that persist for weeks before skin blisters appear.
Flaccid skin blisters filled with clear fluid on normal-appearing or slightly red skin; they rupture very easily with minimal trauma, unlike the tense blisters of pemphigoid.
Widespread raw, weeping erosions where blisters have broken, most prominent on the scalp, face, chest, axillae, groin, and back.
Positive Nikolsky sign — pressing sideways on apparently intact skin adjacent to a blister causes the top layer to shear off, confirming active acantholysis.
Pain and burning at erosion sites that interferes significantly with eating, speaking, and swallowing when oral and pharyngeal mucosa are involved.
Genital and perianal erosions in pemphigus vulgaris that cause pain with urination and defecation.
Crusted, scaling, and greasy lesions on the face, chest, and back in pemphigus foliaceous, often mistaken for seborrheic dermatitis before the fragile blisters are noticed.
Hoarseness or stridor when laryngeal mucosa is involved in pemphigus vulgaris, signalling a potentially serious airway complication.
Weight loss and malnutrition in severe cases where oral erosions prevent adequate food and fluid intake.
Constitutional symptoms of fever, malaise, and anorexia during active disease phases, particularly with secondary infection of the erosions.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent oral ulcers or erosions that do not heal within 2 weeks and cause progressive difficulty eating.
A new blister on normal skin that ruptures with minimal pressure, leaving a raw area instead of healing like a usual wound.
Painful erosion at the edge of previously healed skin when rubbed gently with a finger.
Unexplained sore throat or hoarseness with visible white erosions in the throat in a middle-aged adult.
emergencySigns: Blistering and erosion covering more than 30% of body surface area with signs of secondary infection, dehydration, or sepsis.
Stridor or severe dysphagia indicating laryngeal or oesophageal involvement threatening the airway.
High fever with purulent discharge from erosions suggesting invasive infection — Staphylococcus aureus bacteraemia is a leading cause of pemphigus mortality.
Any features of paraneoplastic pemphigus including respiratory distress from bronchiolitis obliterans.

causes.cause: Autoimmune IgG antibodies against desmoglein 3 (and desmoglein 1)
causes.description: The central mechanism of pemphigus vulgaris; IgG4-dominant autoantibodies bind to the extracellular domain of Dsg3 on keratinocytes, disrupting desmosomal adhesion and triggering intracellular signalling that causes acantholysis — the separation of keratinocytes from each other within the epidermis.

HLA-DRB1*04:02 and HLA-DQB1*05:03 alleles strongly predispose to pemphigus vulgaris in Ashkenazi Jewish individuals; HLA-DQ1 is associated in Japanese populations; these MHC class II molecules are thought to present desmoglein-derived peptides and initiate the autoimmune response.

Drugs containing sulfhydryl groups — notably penicillamine, captopril, and thioproline — modify desmoglein biochemistry directly or activate the immune system, inducing an acantholytic process that clinically resembles idiopathic pemphigus and usually remits when the offending drug is stopped.

UV radiation, thermal burns, and certain infections have been proposed as triggers that unmask latent autoimmunity in genetically susceptible people; the endemic form in Brazil is associated with environmental antigen exposure, possibly a black fly salivary protein that cross-reacts with Dsg1.

In paraneoplastic pemphigus, an underlying malignancy — most commonly non-Hodgkin lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukaemia, or thymoma — generates autoantibodies against multiple epithelial adhesion proteins including periplakin and envoplakin, producing the most severe phenotype.

riskFactors.factor: Ashkenazi Jewish, Middle Eastern, or Indian ancestry
riskFactors.description: HLA haplotypes conferring high risk of pemphigus vulgaris are enriched 5-10 times in Ashkenazi Jewish populations compared with Northern Europeans; Indian and Iranian populations also have elevated incidence driven by shared HLA alleles.
Pemphigus vulgaris peaks in middle age when immune dysregulation and cumulative environmental exposures interact in genetically susceptible individuals.

Thiol drugs are the most established environmental risk factors for drug-induced pemphigus; the risk is proportional to cumulative dose and resolves with drug discontinuation in the majority of cases.
Approximately 80-90% of paraneoplastic pemphigus cases have an associated haematological or solid malignancy; diagnosing and treating the tumour is central to managing the blistering disease.

riskFactors.factor: First-degree relative with autoimmune blistering disease
riskFactors.description: Family clusters of pemphigus have been reported, reflecting shared HLA alleles; the absolute risk to relatives of an affected individual is not precisely quantified but is elevated.
Pemphigus vulgaris affects women slightly more often than men in most populations, consistent with the broader female predominance in organ-specific autoimmune diseases.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in the Cerrado region of Brazil
riskFactors.description: Fogo selvagem (endemic pemphigus foliaceous) is geographically restricted to rural Brazil, with an incidence of up to 3.4 per 1,000 people in hyperendemic communities; migration out of endemic areas reduces risk.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 40-60, who represent the core pemphigus vulgaris demographic in most countries
Ashkenazi Jewish populations worldwide, with an incidence approximately 5-10 times higher than the general European-descent population
People of Middle Eastern and Indian ancestry in whom specific HLA alleles are common
Brazilian rural communities in endemic fogo selvagem zones where all ages including children can be affected
Adults of any age and sex taking penicillamine, captopril, or other thiol medications
Older adults with haematological malignancy who may develop paraneoplastic pemphigus
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis rests on three pillars: characteristic clinical morphology, histopathology, and immunofluorescence. Clinically, the combination of fragile flaccid blisters that rupture on pressure (positive Nikolsky sign), painful oral erosions, and a seborrhoeic distribution raises the suspicion for pemphigus. Skin biopsy for routine H&E histology from the edge of a fresh blister shows intraepidermal splitting with acantholytic cells — the defining feature that distinguishes pemphigus from the subepidermal blistering of pemphigoid. Peri-lesional skin submitted for direct immunofluorescence (DIF) demonstrates the pathognomonic intercellular deposition of IgG and C3 in a chicken-wire pattern throughout the epidermis; DIF is positive in approximately 90-95% of active pemphigus cases. Indirect immunofluorescence (IIIF) on monkey oesophagus and ELISA for anti-Dsg1 and anti-Dsg3 IgG serum antibodies confirm the diagnosis and can be used to monitor disease activity — rising titres correlate with relapse. In suspected paraneoplastic pemphigus, bronchoscopy, CT of chest and abdomen, and bone marrow biopsy are indicated to identify the underlying malignancy. Important differentials include bullous pemphigoid (subepidermal blistering, tense blisters, anti-BP180/BP230 antibodies), dermatitis herpetiformis, erythema multiforme, and severe oral mucositis from chemotherapy.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin biopsy for haematoxylin and eosin histology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates the characteristic intraepidermal split and acantholytic Tzanck cells that define pemphigus and distinguish it from subepidermal blistering diseases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small punch biopsy (3-4 mm) is taken under local anaesthetic from the edge of a fresh blister; the wound is closed with a single suture; results are available in 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects intercellular IgG and C3 deposition in the peri-lesional skin — the gold-standard immunopathological confirmation of pemphigus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A separate 4 mm punch biopsy is taken from clinically normal-appearing skin adjacent to an active lesion; it must be placed in Michel transport medium, not formalin; results take 5-7 days.

diagnosticTests.test: ELISA for anti-Dsg1 and anti-Dsg3 IgG antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies circulating autoantibodies to confirm the diagnosis and to monitor treatment response; rising titres often precede clinical relapse.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw is sent to an immunology laboratory; results return in 1-3 days; titres are tracked serially every 3-6 months during treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates serum antibodies that bind the epidermal intercellular substance of a substrate tissue, confirming the presence of circulating pemphigus antibodies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is taken; the serum is applied to primate tissue slides in a specialist lab and read by a pathologist.

diagnosticTests.test: Nikolsky sign assessment and clinical examination
diagnosticTests.purpose: Positive lateral pressure shearing of intact skin adjacent to a blister supports active acantholysis and is performed at every clinical visit to assess disease activity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician applies gentle lateral pressure with a finger to apparently normal skin; skin that shears off in sheets indicates active disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count, metabolic panel, and glucose
diagnosticTests.purpose: Baseline assessment before corticosteroid initiation; monitors for corticosteroid-related metabolic effects, bone marrow suppression from azathioprine or mycophenolate, and infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; repeated at 4-6 week intervals during treatment adjustment.

treatmentOverview: Pemphigus requires long-term systemic immunosuppression managed by a dermatologist. The 2020 S2k European guidelines and international consensus recommend rituximab plus short-course oral prednisone as the preferred first-line treatment for moderate-to-severe pemphigus vulgaris, based on the Ritux 3 randomised trial showing superior remission rates compared with prednisone alone. Rituximab, an anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody, depletes B cells and interrupts autoantibody production; a protocol of two 1,000 mg IV infusions separated by 2 weeks produces complete remission off corticosteroids in approximately 90% of patients at 2 years. For patients in whom rituximab is unavailable or contraindicated, high-dose oral prednisolone (0.5-1.5 mg/kg/day) combined with a corticosteroid-sparing agent — mycophenolate mofetil 2-3 g/day, azathioprine 2.5 mg/kg/day, or dapsone — remains effective. Corticosteroids are tapered slowly once new blister formation ceases, typically over 9-18 months. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) and plasmapheresis are reserved for severe refractory cases. Wound care is central: erosions are dressed with non-adherent dressings, and secondary bacterial infection — most commonly with Staphylococcus aureus — is treated promptly with systemic antibiotics when clinical signs of infection are present. Topical antiseptic washes and emollients reduce infection risk. Pain management using analgesic mouthwashes, topical anaesthetics, and systemic analgesia is important for maintaining nutrition when oral erosions are active. Serum Dsg antibody titres are measured every 3-6 months to guide corticosteroid tapering and to identify imminent relapse.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab (anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody)
medicalTreatments.description: Two IV infusions of 1,000 mg separated by 2 weeks (rheumatoid arthritis protocol) deplete peripheral B cells for 6-9 months, eliminating the plasma cell precursors that produce pathogenic IgG anti-desmoglein antibodies; infusions are administered in hospital with monitoring for infusion reactions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete remission off all treatment achieved in approximately 89% of patients at 24 months in the Ritux 3 RCT, compared with 34% for prednisone alone; now endorsed as first-line in international guidelines.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisolone
medicalTreatments.description: Still used alongside rituximab as a short-course bridge and as primary therapy where rituximab is unavailable; starting dose 0.5-1.5 mg/kg/day, tapered as new blister formation ceases; cumulative dose should be minimised to reduce metabolic, skeletal, and infectious complications.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Arrests new blister formation in approximately 80-90% of patients within 2-4 weeks; disease control without rituximab requires continuation for 12-24 months with slow tapering.

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil 2-3 g/day
medicalTreatments.description: A purine synthesis inhibitor that suppresses T and B lymphocyte proliferation; used as a corticosteroid-sparing agent allowing faster prednisone taper while maintaining disease control; preferred over azathioprine in many centres for its more favourable adverse-effect profile.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adds approximately 40-50% corticosteroid dose reduction versus prednisone alone in pemphigus; complete remission rates of 60-70% at 12 months in combination regimens.

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine 2.5 mg/kg/day
medicalTreatments.description: An older corticosteroid-sparing immunosuppressant; requires TPMT genotyping or enzyme activity testing before starting to avoid severe bone marrow suppression in poor metabolisers; effective but takes 6-12 weeks to reach full effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cumulative corticosteroid dose by approximately 30-40% and lowers relapse rate compared with corticosteroids alone in controlled trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG)
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose IVIG (2 g/kg per cycle) rapidly reduces circulating anti-desmoglein antibody titres through Fc-receptor-mediated catabolism and idiotype–anti-idiotype networks; reserved for severe acute disease or patients with contraindications to immunosuppression including active infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves meaningful clinical response in approximately 65-75% of refractory pemphigus patients in case series; effect is short-lived (4-8 weeks) without concurrent immunosuppression.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dapsone
alternativeTreatments.description: An anti-neutrophil agent sometimes used as adjunct therapy in pemphigus foliaceous and mild pemphigus vulgaris; requires G6PD testing before use to prevent haemolysis; generally used as a third-line corticosteroid-sparing agent.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids and antiseptic wound care
alternativeTreatments.description: Clobetasol propionate 0.05% cream applied to isolated skin lesions and antiseptic washes (diluted bleach baths or chlorhexidine) reduce bacterial colonisation of erosions; used as adjuncts to systemic therapy, not substitutes.

whySeeSpecialist: Pemphigus requires management by a dermatologist with experience in autoimmune blistering diseases. The diagnosis hinges on histopathology and immunofluorescence that must be performed correctly, and the treatment — systemic immunosuppression including biologics — carries significant risks requiring monitoring. Patients with oral involvement should also see an oral medicine specialist; laryngeal disease needs otolaryngology input; and ocular involvement requires ophthalmologic evaluation. Suspected paraneoplastic pemphigus mandates urgent oncological assessment.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a dermatologist who subspecialises in immunobullous or autoimmune skin diseases, ideally at a centre where both direct immunofluorescence and anti-desmoglein ELISA are performed in-house. Access to rituximab infusion and to dermatopathology expertise is important. Ask whether the dermatologist works in a multidisciplinary team with oral medicine, ophthalmology, and immunology. For monitoring, regular anti-Dsg antibody titres should be part of the follow-up protocol.
preventionStrategies: For patients on penicillamine or captopril who develop oral ulcers or skin blisters, report to the prescribing physician immediately as drug withdrawal usually resolves drug-induced pemphigus.
Once in remission, maintain regular follow-up even when asymptomatic, as serum antibody titres often rise before clinical relapse, allowing treatment to be adjusted preemptively.
All patients on corticosteroids long-term should receive bone protection with calcium, vitamin D, and bisphosphonate therapy, and should be screened for diabetes and hypertension.
Avoid trauma to the skin — adhesive tapes, friction, pressure, and minor burns can extend erosions in active pemphigus.
lifestyleModifications: Use gentle, fragrance-free soaps and moisturisers; avoid products that disrupt the skin barrier.
Wear loose, soft, seamless clothing to minimise friction on fragile skin and healing erosions.
Maintain good oral hygiene with a soft toothbrush and antiseptic mouthwash to reduce secondary infection in oral erosions.
Eat soft, non-abrasive foods — mashed, pureed, and liquid diets — when oral erosions are active; use a dietitian for nutritional support if intake is severely restricted.
Avoid known contact triggers for drug-induced disease; carry a medication allergy list listing penicillamine and thiol compounds.
Use high-SPF sunscreen and avoid intense sun exposure, particularly in pemphigus foliaceous where UV light can flare the disease.
Maintain emotional and psychological support — chronic painful blistering diseases carry a high burden of depression and anxiety; psychological referral should be offered proactively.

recommended: Soft, nutritious foods that minimise trauma to oral erosions — yoghurt, smoothies, scrambled eggs, mashed potato, soups
High-protein intake to support wound healing of erosions — eggs, dairy, lean meat, or plant protein supplements
Calcium (1,000-1,200 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-1,000 IU/day) supplementation for all patients on systemic corticosteroids
Fluid-rich foods and adequate hydration to compensate for fluid loss from extensive erosions
avoid: Hard, crunchy, acidic, or spicy foods that traumatise or irritate oral erosions
Alcohol, which interacts with methotrexate and impairs wound healing
Excess salt and sugar during corticosteroid therapy to mitigate weight gain, hypertension, and glucose intolerance

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is encouraged during stable remission for general wellbeing and to counteract corticosteroid-related weight gain and muscle wasting. During active disease with extensive erosions, strenuous activity that causes sweating and friction should be minimised; low-impact activities such as walking and gentle swimming in clean, chlorinated pools are generally appropriate. Contact sports and activities risking skin trauma should be avoided until erosions are fully healed.
dailyManagement: Inspect skin and mucous membranes daily for new blisters and note their location and size to report at the next clinic visit.
Apply prescribed topical therapy to lesional skin and use non-adherent wound dressings on open erosions, changing dressings as directed.
Take all prescribed medications at the correct time; never stop corticosteroids or immunosuppressants without medical guidance.
Monitor for signs of infection — increasing warmth, redness, swelling, or purulent discharge from erosions — and contact the dermatologist or GP promptly.
Attend all scheduled blood tests for monitoring complete blood count, liver and renal function, and anti-desmoglein antibody titres.
Use prescribed analgesic mouthwashes before meals to manage oral pain and maintain nutritional intake.
prognosis: With modern treatment, particularly rituximab, the prognosis of pemphigus vulgaris has improved markedly. Complete remission off all therapy is achieved in approximately 40-70% of patients within 2 years of rituximab-based treatment, compared with historically high mortality before immunosuppressive therapy existed. The disease is however chronic and relapsing in many patients; approximately 30-40% relapse after initial remission and require re-treatment. Mortality, previously as high as 75%, now ranges from 5-10% in treated patients and is most often due to sepsis from secondary infection of erosions or from immunosuppression-related complications. Pemphigus foliaceous has a better prognosis than pemphigus vulgaris with lower mortality and a higher rate of spontaneous remission in some cases. Paraneoplastic pemphigus carries a poor prognosis, with 5-year survival rates below 50%, largely due to the underlying malignancy and respiratory complications.
recoveryTimeline: After starting rituximab, new blister formation typically ceases within 4-8 weeks, and most erosions heal within 3-4 months. Complete clinical remission is achieved in the majority of patients within 6-12 months. Corticosteroid tapering usually begins once no new blisters have formed for 2-4 weeks, with the taper proceeding over 6-18 months depending on antibody titres and clinical response. Patients should expect continued follow-up for 2-3 years after apparent remission, as late relapses can occur.
complications: Secondary bacterial infection — Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa are the most common colonisers of pemphigus erosions; invasive infection including sepsis is the leading cause of death in active pemphigus.

Corticosteroid toxicity — long-term prednisolone use causes weight gain, diabetes mellitus, hypertension, osteoporosis, avascular necrosis of bone, and increased susceptibility to opportunistic infections.
Herpes simplex superinfection (Kaposi's varicelliform eruption) — HSV can spread across pemphigus erosions, producing a clinical emergency requiring prompt IV aciclovir.
Scarring and post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation — healing erosions in darker skin types frequently leave prolonged pigmentation change, though scarring is uncommon in classic pemphigus.
Nutritional deficiency — severe oral and oesophageal involvement can prevent adequate intake, leading to weight loss, hypoalbuminaemia, and micronutrient deficiency.
Psychological morbidity — chronic disfiguring disease with unpredictable flares is associated with clinically significant rates of depression and anxiety, which independently worsen disease outcomes.

supportResources.name: International Pemphigus & Pemphigoid Foundation (IPPF)
supportResources.description: Leading patient advocacy organisation for pemphigus and pemphigoid; offers a peer health coach programme, educational webinars, and support groups.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — Pemphigus vulgaris Patient Leaflet
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on pemphigus vulgaris symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment from the UK dermatology professional body.

supportResources.name: NIAMS — Pemphigus
supportResources.description: U.S. National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases overview of pemphigus for patients.

sources.title: Joly P et al. First-Line Rituximab Combined with Short-Term Prednisone versus Prednisone Alone for the Treatment of Pemphigus (Ritux 3). Lancet. 2017;389(10083):2031-2040.
Murrell DF et al. Diagnosis and management of pemphigus: Recommendations of an international panel of experts. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2020;82(3):575-585.
Kasperkiewicz M et al. Pemphigus. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2017;3:17026.
British Association of Dermatologists (BAD) — Pemphigus vulgaris patient leaflet.
NIAMS — Pemphigus overview. National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases, NIH.
International Pemphigus & Pemphigoid Foundation — What is Pemphigus?
Amagai M. Pemphigus as a paradigm of autoimmunity and cell adhesion. Keio J Med. 2002;51(3):133-139.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bullous pemphigoid
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bullous pemphigoid produces tense blisters that are much harder to rupture, occurs in older adults (>70 years), rarely involves mucous membranes severely, and shows subepidermal rather than intraepidermal blistering with anti-BP180/BP230 antibodies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dermatitis herpetiformis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Dermatitis herpetiformis produces intensely itchy, grouped tiny blisters over extensor surfaces, is associated with coeliac disease and anti-tTG antibodies, and shows subepidermal IgA deposits — entirely different immunopathology from pemphigus.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema causes itchy, dry, weeping inflamed skin but does not produce intraepidermal blisters that separate under pressure or show the intercellular IgG pattern on immunofluorescence.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis presents with scaling, erythematous plaques without blistering, and oral involvement is rare; the Nikolsky sign is negative, and immunofluorescence is normal.

relatedConditions.name: Bullous pemphigoid
relatedConditions.relevance: The most important differential diagnosis; both are autoimmune blistering diseases but with different cleavage levels, antibody targets, demographics, and treatment responses.

relatedConditions.name: Pemphigus vulgaris
relatedConditions.relevance: Pemphigus vulgaris is the most common specific subtype encompassed by the unspecified pemphigus diagnosis; understanding its features drives diagnostic evaluation.

relatedConditions.name: Dermatitis herpetiformis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another autoimmune blistering disease that must be distinguished from pemphigus by histopathology and immunofluorescence; managed with dapsone and a gluten-free diet.

relatedConditions.name: Paraneoplastic pemphigus
relatedConditions.relevance: The most severe pemphigus variant, which arises in the context of malignancy and requires urgent oncological investigation.

faqs.question: What is pemphigus?
faqs.answer: Pemphigus is a rare autoimmune disease where the immune system makes antibodies that attack the proteins holding skin cells together, causing painful blisters and erosions on the skin and inside the mouth. It is not contagious and is not caused by an allergy or infection.

faqs.question: What does pemphigus look like?
faqs.answer: Pemphigus vulgaris typically starts with painful sores and erosions inside the mouth, then develops fragile blisters on the skin that burst easily, leaving raw, weeping patches. Pemphigus foliaceous causes scaling, crusty, flaky lesions on the face, chest, and back without mouth involvement.

faqs.question: Is pemphigus curable?
faqs.answer: Pemphigus cannot always be permanently cured, but modern treatment with rituximab achieves complete remission off all medication in approximately 40-70% of patients. Many others achieve long-term control with maintenance therapy. The disease may relapse, but most patients lead a near-normal life with appropriate management.

faqs.question: What is the difference between pemphigus and pemphigoid?
faqs.answer: Both are autoimmune blistering diseases, but they differ in the layer of skin where blistering occurs and who they affect. Pemphigus causes blisters within the epidermis that rupture very easily, affects adults aged 40-60, and often involves the mouth. Pemphigoid causes tense blisters beneath the epidermis, affects older adults over 70, and rarely causes severe mouth disease.

faqs.question: How is pemphigus diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a skin biopsy showing intraepidermal splitting, a separate biopsy for direct immunofluorescence demonstrating IgG deposits between skin cells in a chicken-wire pattern, and a blood test for anti-desmoglein 1 and 3 antibodies. All three pieces of evidence together confirm the diagnosis.

faqs.question: What causes pemphigus?
faqs.answer: Pemphigus is caused by IgG antibodies produced by the immune system against desmoglein proteins that glue skin cells together. Genetic factors — particularly certain HLA gene variants — predispose people to making these antibodies. Certain drugs including penicillamine can also trigger pemphigus.

faqs.question: How is pemphigus treated?
faqs.answer: The current first-line treatment is rituximab — two infusions given 2 weeks apart — combined with a short course of prednisolone tablets. Rituximab depletes the B cells that make the harmful antibodies and produces complete remission in around 9 out of 10 patients within 2 years in clinical trials.

faqs.question: Is pemphigus fatal?
faqs.answer: Pemphigus was often fatal before modern treatments, with up to 75% mortality. Today, with corticosteroids and rituximab, mortality has fallen to approximately 5-10%, mainly from secondary infection or complications of immunosuppression. Early diagnosis and treatment are key to the best outcomes.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid with pemphigus?
faqs.answer: Avoid hard, crunchy, acidic, or spicy foods when mouth sores are active as they worsen pain and slow healing. Soft foods — yoghurt, soups, mashed vegetables, smoothies — are easier to manage. During corticosteroid treatment, minimise salt, sugar, and alcohol to reduce side effects such as weight gain and high blood pressure.

faqs.question: Can pemphigus affect the mouth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Oral erosions are the first sign of pemphigus vulgaris in about 50-70% of patients, sometimes appearing months before skin blisters. The gums, palate, cheeks, and tongue develop painful raw areas that make eating and speaking difficult. Untreated mouth disease contributes to weight loss and nutritional deficiency.

faqs.question: Is pemphigus hereditary?
faqs.answer: Pemphigus is not directly inherited in a simple Mendelian pattern, but genetic susceptibility genes — particularly HLA alleles — are passed down through families. Relatives of affected individuals have a slightly elevated risk. Most people with the relevant HLA genes do not develop pemphigus, which means additional environmental or immune factors are required.

faqs.question: What is the Nikolsky sign?
faqs.answer: The Nikolsky sign is a clinical test where gentle sideways pressure on seemingly normal skin adjacent to a blister causes the top skin layer to shear off. A positive sign indicates active acantholysis — the breakdown of cell adhesion within the epidermis — and strongly supports a diagnosis of pemphigus over other blistering conditions.

faqs.question: How long does pemphigus treatment last?
faqs.answer: Treatment duration varies. After rituximab, corticosteroids are usually tapered over 6-18 months. Patients are monitored for at least 2-3 years after remission because late relapses can occur. Some patients achieve long-term remission off all treatment; others need ongoing maintenance immunosuppression to prevent recurrence.

faqs.question: What is paraneoplastic pemphigus?
faqs.answer: Paraneoplastic pemphigus is a severe variant of pemphigus associated with an underlying cancer, most often lymphoma. It produces extensive mucocutaneous blistering and a life-threatening lung complication called bronchiolitis obliterans. Finding and treating the underlying cancer is central to management.

faqs.question: Can pemphigus affect the eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly cicatricial pemphigoid (a related condition) more commonly causes eye scarring. Pemphigus vulgaris can affect conjunctival mucosa in severe cases, but ocular involvement is less prominent than in pemphigoid. Any eye symptoms — pain, redness, or vision change — in a pemphigus patient should be assessed by an ophthalmologist.

faqs.question: Can stress trigger pemphigus flares?
faqs.answer: Psychological stress is reported by many patients as a trigger for flares, and some immunological studies support a link between stress hormones and autoimmune activity. While the evidence is not definitive, stress management strategies are a reasonable component of overall disease management.

faqs.question: What is drug-induced pemphigus?
faqs.answer: Certain drugs — especially penicillamine and captopril, which contain thiol groups — can trigger pemphigus by interfering with desmosome proteins or activating the autoimmune response. Drug-induced pemphigus usually improves or resolves after the offending drug is stopped, sometimes without further treatment.

faqs.question: How do doctors monitor pemphigus during treatment?
faqs.answer: Monitoring includes regular slit-lamp skin inspection for new blisters, serial measurement of anti-desmoglein 1 and 3 antibody titres every 3-6 months, and blood tests to detect immunosuppression side effects — complete blood count, liver function, renal function, and glucose.

faqs.question: Can children get pemphigus?
faqs.answer: Pemphigus vulgaris is rare in children but is documented. Fogo selvagem (Brazilian endemic pemphigus foliaceous) can affect children and adolescents in hyperendemic regions. When a child develops unexplained oral erosions or fragile skin blisters, a dermatology referral and biopsy are warranted.

faqs.question: What is the role of IVIG in pemphigus treatment?
faqs.answer: Intravenous immunoglobulin is used for severe acute pemphigus or when standard immunosuppression is contraindicated. High-dose IVIG rapidly lowers circulating anti-desmoglein antibody levels and can produce rapid improvement in blister burden, but the effect is temporary and it is usually combined with longer-term immunosuppression.

metaDescription: Pemphigus is an autoimmune blistering disease causing fragile skin blisters and painful oral erosions. Learn causes, diagnosis, and rituximab treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
